comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's almost midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Llewellyn George, one of early 20th century America's great astrologers and teachers of astrology. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You mentioned getting occasional visions of the future. What are they, and how did you reach the point you'd get them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The visions I've had are spontaneous; they're not things I set out to get. They just happened. The first round came while I was doing the kind of very boring job that requires concentration without thinking. That was in 1989, and the fascinating thing about those visions is that they turned out to be wrong. I can see how things could have worked out that way, but events took a different path.

Since then, I've had a variety of visions now and again. What interests me is that by and large I can feel that they're probabilities, not certainties. For example, for a while there I got a lot of images of civil war, but that's receded sharply since 2016; my sense is that we're not out of the woods yet but it's likely that we'll get past without that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I may ask, what were the first round of visions? Also, are visions of the future a typical development from intensive magical practice, or is it more personal?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The first round involved a long and highly complex timeline; the short form was that a major economic crisis in 2004-2006 was followed by a temporary reprieve, and then by some much more serious crisis -- not wholly economic in nature -- that hit in 2012 and caused a general unraveling of US society.

As far as I can tell, you either get visions of the future or you don't, and magical practice doesn't have much to do with it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Are your visions from your point of view, or from something else?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not at all. They're very difficult to describe -- "visions" is actually the wrong word for them, strictly speaking, as they have very little visual component. I experience a very precise emotional tone corresponding to the "feeling" of the time, which I can only compare to the state left behind after reading a very good novel, and little nonverbal intuitive flashes that give details. None of it is from anyone's point of view -- it's more an overview, as though I was reading a history written in some peculiar nonverbal language (if that makes any sense at all).

replies.title: Future vision
replies.content:
JMG,

Re visions of potential futures - I'm wondering if that wasn't the case with some of the spectacularly failed predictions of Edgar Cayce. He predicted such things as a severe Europe and Japanese territorial rearranging via earthquake at a time when the fascist powers were in ascent in the late '30's and early 40's. I'm thinking that when Cayce made these predictions, there might have been a definite potential for such a geological upheaval if the Axis Powers had indeed triumphed in the long run.

Andectdotally, I remember the day that Elizabeth Clare Prophet had predicted to be the day of world-wide nuclear holocaust. Actually I didn't know the particular date - it was a day in March, 1990, I think - but I did know that she had predicted such a day and that she and her followers took it seriously enough that they began digging fallout shelters in Montana. The day in question was very peculiar; there was a feeling of a windy emptiness, of an apocalyptic finality, a grand, sweeping, final chord conclusion. Hard to describe, but it was a distinct perception. I later found out that the day had been the one ECP had predicted as nuclear holocaust day. ECP was on to something, I think, if not the event of a nuclear apocalypse, then the potentiality of one - or perhaps she had sensed a major shift on the upper planes and it translated as a nuclear apocalypse. - thanks, Will M

replies.title: Re: Future vision
replies.content: I always figured that Cayce mistook changes in consciousness for changes in landscape. He predicted, for example, that Atlantis would rise from the sea in 1968 or 1969; it didn't, but he got the timing of the great occult boom of the late 1960s and 1970s dead on, and one of the books that kickstarted that boom was, you guessed it, John Michell's The View Over Atlantis.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: For what it's worth, the only time in my life I've had precognitive dreams was during a period of several months that I was regularly practicing the Celtic Golden Dawn material. I haven't had any since I stopped three years ago.

replies.title: Visions
replies.content:
That corresponds very much with what Michael has been saying. A civil war was not only a definite possibility up to the 2016 Presidential election; in parallels where Clinton won, the nation moved very quickly toward civil war. The people who wanted the civil war thought they got what they wanted with Trump. The probability hasn't completely vanished, but it's fairly low at present.

John Roth

replies.title: Re: Visions
replies.content: That's very much my read on it. If Trump loses in 2020, and there's serious evidence of fraud, things could blow up very quickly; barring that, I suspect we've dodged that particular bullet.

replies.title: Re: Visions
replies.content: From your mouth to Tyche's ears.

replies.title: probabilities
replies.content:
"they're probabilities, not certainties"
This immediately brought to mind the futures seen by Paul Atreides in the book Dune, as multiple probabilities based on key decisions.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
" The picture is of Llewellyn George, one of early 20th century America's great astrologers and teachers of astrology."

Any relation between him and the publishing company Llewellyn?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: George founded the company. After his death it was picked up by a California firm for a while, then bought by Carl Weschke, who changed his middle name to Llewellyn and moved the firm to the Minneapolis area.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Will J here:

This is a bit of an odd thought, but given "What you contemplate, you imitate", given the way so many pagans seem to see Christian influence everywhere (they're not wrong, but still), is it possible that Paganism is copying Christianity?

On a related note, how do you contemplate something you need to think about without copying it? Is it a matter of finding something else to think about that's suitably different? I'm still finding it useful to mull over Faustian culture, but I want to avoid being drawn further into it, so would extensive study of Classical culture help avoid that risk, or is there something else that would work better?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Make sure you mull over a variety of other cultures too. Apollonian culture is one good choice, but it would be good to learn something about at least one other, too, and put some time into absorbing that influence.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Well, I've always been interested in Chinese culture, so I guess it's time to start looking for sources.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey jmg
In my reading on the golden dawn many of my books say that the neophyte has to perform a special neophyte self initiation ritual. What I want to know is..
1- the self initiation ritual requires the magic tools that from what I gather your only supposed to make once you have reached a higher grade. How does the neophyte perform a ritual that requires tools that they are no ready to make?
2- is a self initiation ritual even necessary? You don’t seem to talk about them at all in your books and posts on the golden dawn.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It depends on what kind of self-initiation ritual we're discussing. Israel Regardie gives an excellent ritual in his book Ceremonial Magic that involves no magical working tools at all.

2) No, a specific ritual isn't necessary, though it can be helpful. If you're doing the basic practices of the tradition -- LBRP and Middle Pillar, meditation, and divination -- and adding in further workings as you develop the necessary skill, that's your self-initiation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks, I’ll look up that regardie book!

comments.title: Tropical Ovate Tree Working
comments.content:
I live in Hawaii and obviously our trees can be very different from those mentioned in your books. I'm not sure if you would know which is safe for tree working but figured I might ask anyway. Nearby I have a noni, a mango, a breadfruit, and a couple huge albizias. There is also pine but it's next to a highway and doesn't look too happy. Pines and albizias are invasive here so not sure if it's okay to 'support' them from an ethical standpoint and I'd rather find something native to work with if possible. Also the be breadfruit and mango will need to be trimmed soon so I'm wondering if that makes a difference as well. I would mention coconut trees but as everyone knows (or should know) around here those things are really dangerous and that's the last place you want to be standing!

Thanks for doing this every week btw. Aloha from the swaying palms.

replies.title: Re: Tropical Ovate Tree Working
replies.content: Never having been to Hawaii, I have no idea. You'll want to experiment; do the tree working, and see how you feel afterwards. A successful tree working should leave you feeling energized and calm.

replies.title: Re: Tropical Ovate Tree Working
replies.content:
Okay will give it a try. Noni is a medicinal tree, and many older residents here regularly have a glass jar with a few fruits fermenting in the sun in their front yards. It tastes awful and smells worse but they swear by it.

Is the tree working ruined if I chainsaw a couple branches right after? I try to leave at least 2/3 of the canopy so that they can recover quickly.

replies.title: Re: Tropical Ovate Tree Working
replies.content: Ask the tree. Most trees don't like being cut, but they'll tolerate it if it's for their benefit.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Are things which are eternal capable of change? If so, it's hard to figure out how, but if not, then wouldn't spiritual growth stop once a being reaches an eternal plane?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Questions like that are more about our capacity to understand the cosmos than they are about the cosmos. Since you don't have to worry about that for many, many lives, I'd suggest waiting until you get close to that point and asking the beings you'll encounter at that time.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
This is probably not very magical, in and of itself, but may be useful to someone's work. I've been reading the color correspondences with interest in various comments here over the last few months.

Yesterday was Christ the King, a shift in liturgical color from the green of Ordinary Time to red. Next week we go to purple for Advent, except for the week that's rose, then white at Christmas.

(For non-liturgical Christians and non-Christians, this is the color of all the cloths, stoles, etc, in the Church. You'll see it on the altar, the banners, the pastor, the acolytes, and so on. Also in the Advent Wreath candles. Some of us in the pews even wear the liturgical color. Next Sunday, by the way, is the start of the Christian Year, so you can confuse many by wishing them Happy New Year, if you enjoy that sort of thing.)

I've been looking at Catholic and Orthodox websites, as there's not much on the liturgical Protestents' pages, and of course I know some color symbolism from Job's Daughters and Eastern Star, but can anyone suggest a good inclusive encyclopedia of color symbolism? Or is this something that needs to be written?

Thanks much,
BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know of any such book, and I'd like to preorder a copy!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: BoysMom: Just an aside: While we do follow a similar pattern of color changes (purple during the great fasts, green during ordinary time, etc), the Orthodox liturgical year begins in September ;).

comments.title: counter-spelling and poetry
comments.content:
You mentioned here that frontal resistance is the weakest form of defense.

Given that,ive been thinking about the implications of this in magical defense.

Say, if someone had placed a bad intention upon me, and i perform a counterspell, am i correct in asuming that an intention focused in making the bad intention irrelevant is more likely to succeed than a simple negation?

If that is correct, i will very much see how poetry makes good magic.

And then, about blowbacks. If i intend to untangle the knots of confusion bringed by someone, i assume i will get also the hard work of untangling my own.Correct?

Also, if you had any other coment on those matters it will be much apreciated.

Many thanks,

Guillem

replies.title: Re: counter-spelling and poetry
replies.content: Correct on both counts. You may find in the second case, though, that your counterspell helps you untangle the confusion -- by the Raspberry Jam Principle, if you're invoking energies of clarity and insight into yourself, they're going to affect everything in your life, and various puzzles and perplexities unrelated to the spell may suddenly become clear!

replies.title: Re: counter-spelling and poetry
replies.content: That sounds promising.Many thanks.

replies.title: Re: counter-spelling and poetry
replies.content:
Hmm... Would you say it is proper to apply the principles of a physical conflict to a magical conflict? If you apply a kind of escalation scheme to the case of physical violence, usually the best thing to do is to sense and avoid situations that might place you under threat. If such a situation cannot be avoided, of course it is better to get out of the situation without physical contact. If even that is not possible, depending on your skill and experience you can opt for a variety of measures. My point is: The better you are equipped for the last step of this scheme, the less you will need it (unless you are somebody who WANTS the trouble). If you are well equipped for a physical fight and did not neglect the necessary mental development, you suffer less fear, you can better estimate the situation, the opponent, the possible blow back of what you might want to do, etc.

Linking this back to magic: Is there a kind of magical sparring? If so, would it have the same benefit like its physical counterpart? How would you do it?

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: counter-spelling and poetry
replies.content:
I don't know of a magical equivalent of sparring practice, but if you're a public figure in the occult community you can expect to come in for enough magical nastiness to get plenty of practice in dealing with it.

I knew a martial arts teacher in Seattle who used to have his black belt candidates go "trolling for muggers" -- that is, walking through a dangerous neighborhood looking lost, with a $50 bill sticking half out of a back pocket. It was good training, but he had to have his students switch neighborhoods pretty often because the muggers got tired of having the bejesus pounded out of them. The same principle seems to apply in magical contexts as well. ;-)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've had a number of dreams over the years that are odd in that they are sort of geometric, or like a vision of multidimensional figures that are cascading about, making the larger objects that we see as physical objects. It is difficult to describe because these seem more like concepts than physical beings like atomic particles and feels a bit outside of time, and I also get this strange sense of Lovecraftian horror (I experience them as semi-nightmares) that fills me with revulsion or something similar, though upon waking I can't explain why.

All this to say, I had a thought recently that I was experiencing the mental plane, and I'm wondering if this is absurd or plausible. I'm not sure why we seem to only experience the astral plane when we sleep. Or maybe this doesn't even sound like the mental plane to you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know what it is, but I too get occasional flashes of that just before I fall asleep proper when I'm off to bed in certain mental states, usually after a long math session. I don't find it terrifying but it is certainly out of my reach. I'd be interested in our hosts take on it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That sounds charmingly like the other-dimensional space Lovecraft describes in "The Dreams in the Witch-House." It doesn't fit my experience of the mental plane, but it may well be a reflection of mental patterns in the upper astral.

We experience the astral plane when we dream because that's what dreaming is -- the mind, during sleep, settles back into its normal place between the astral and spiritual planes, and alternates between perceiving the spiritual plane (this is dreamless sleep) and perceiving the astral plane (dreaming sleep). Because the mental body in human beings isn't yet fully formed -- we have what's called a "mental sheath" rather than a fully developed mental body, like our astral, etheric, and physical bodies -- we wake with vague confused memories of our astral experiences and, usually, no memory at all of our spiritual experiences. As each of us completes the process of human evolution and develops a complete mental body, that changes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Maybe the old expression that newborns smile in sleep (long before they do while awake) because they're talking to angels is somewhat accurate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There may be something to that. It's a common teaching in a variety of occult traditions that until they've been incarnate for a year or two, infants aren't entirely anchored into the physical body, and haven't lost contact with the spiritual realms.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: IF the mind's normal place is between the astral and spiritual plane, as we build a mental body, would our minds settle in the mental plane during sleep, or is there something else that happens?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Once the mental sheath becomes a mental body, sure. It would then be aware of the mental plane, and could turn its attention at will to either the astral or the spiritual planes. Lucid dreaming is a very faint and imperfect echo of what that state of consciousness will be like.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Do you think our lack of a mental body is related to the fact we are vulnerable to demons?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. I don't happen to know.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On the subject of demons, consider this:

https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2018/12/catholic-exorcisms-on-the-rise/573943/

The reporter got right a teaching of a major religion (she’ll probably be fired). The Church has always maintained that fiddling with the occult is dangerous because it attracts demons. Our host has maintained that fiddling with the occult is dangerous unless you do the appropriate protective actions. Interstingly, these positions aren’t all that far apart.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have my disagreements with the Catholic church, but there's a lot of wisdom in some aspects of Catholic teaching.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Speaking of demons, you've said that wallowing in selfish/self-destructive emotional states would let them reach us. Since that seems like a fair description of our society right now, do you think there are a lot more demonic incidents these days than say twenty years ago?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd bet on it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Tying in with the above question about visions of futures, my family actually dreams futures. Maybe because we have the stories about it passed on, so we're open to the possibility, or maybe it's something else. I just tell the kids, "Look, this is a thing, it's not the One True Future, so you just make notes about it and consider it like a weather warning-if you dream a bad thing, keep your eyes open for the start of it so you can duck. Oh, and don't TALK to people outside our family about it. It's just weird enough to freak them out."

BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Ha! I'll have to check that out, thanks. Your explanation makes sense; do you have any recommended reading for exploring some of these finer points of philosophy regarding the planes and the human body?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Dion Fortune covers it in some of her essays, but those are scattered pretty thoroughly through the various published collections of her work. Max Heindel's doorstop-sized The Rosicrucian Cosmo-Conception is the one readily available source I know of, but it's got a lot of extraneous material in it. I'm not at all sure what to recommend.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In addition to the parallels in Lovecraft which our host just cited, Malidoma Somé claims to have had much the same sort of experience during one phase of his tribal initiation. (He's an African from the Dagara tribe of Burkina Faso.) See his "Of Water and the Spirit: Ritual, Magic, and Initiation" (1994).

Caution: Somé is worth reading, even though his two larger books are clearly pandering to Western New-Age expectations. They also record some interesting aspects of his African experience with initiation and magic in general. The real problem is to separate the wheat from the chaff in this book.

Somé's first [quite thin] book, "Ritual: Power, Healing and Community" (1993) is much more thoughtful and well worth the time it takes to read it. I got a lot out of it about ritual practice and power.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks! I'll have to look that up.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I often have dreams that are similarly...architectural, if you will. They usually start in a familiar place but the place expands and becomes quite complex, origami-like, and often very beautiful. I typically experience them as gorgeous and enjoyable. Other members of my family (my mother, my sister, a cousin) also describe dreams such as these. Most of us have jobs that require 3 dimensional thinking (engineer, architect, choreographer, etc.) and I always thought it was just something about how our brains were structured.

Of course, this could mean that our brains have a specific sort of connection to the mental plane, maybe? Can you elaborate on the horror of it--is it something about the shapes themselves that is horrifying? (I've had these as fever dreams) Or is it the action/plot of the dream itself? Or are they inseparable?

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Architectural is not a bad word for it, though I haven't had one for a while so I can't be sure. They do seem to manifest as fever dreams, or at least feel that way, and the attached emotions may be generally related to my physical condition. I can't explain the revulsion or horror, because there is no plot, only sensation, but the best way I could describe it is to point to when we look at large amounts of data and say "It boggles the mind". My mind feels sort of boggled when I experience this. Or it may be the cold, machinelike, inhumanity of the sensations.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My dreams are quite different- they often have me just wandering around, exploring various neighborhoods. Sometimes, I'll be having an adventure, other times it'll be another mundane day.

On the topic of dreams:
Anyone have any experience with lucid dreaming? What is our hosts' opinion of lucid dreaming?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've had a few spontaneous lucid dreams, and I'm pretty sure -- based on hints in occult literature -- that that's what all our dreams will be like once we've finished establishing a mental body.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is basically just for you, JMG, because I know how much you like random data points (or perhaps case studies).

I have had a few lucid dreams, and I tend to find them a sort of dead end, (OK, I know I’m dreaming, now what?) except on the few occasions when I turned to some spiritual pursuit while lucid (then suddenly it’s like the whole landscape changes to a beautiful spiritual landscape). But I don’t think that’s really the end point.

I have had other dreams that were not traditionally lucid, but instead lucid to the relevant archetype. (Dreaming but not suddenly realizing I’m dreaming, but suddenly realizing I’m dancing with my Shadow Self). The closest thing I can compare it to is watching a movie, being totally immersed in it, then suddenly realizing it’s a romance and that means the two protagonists will fall in love and get married. (Or suddenly realizing its a sports film and the protagonist underdogs are going to win the big game).

I have, much more rarely, had similar experiences in waking life. Based on what you just said, I speculate that maybe a fully developed mental body will result in seeing the broader spiritual aspects and archetypes while both asleep and awake. To put it another way, waking from our current reality-dreams to see that all our experiences are part of these soul stories, giving us the ability to act out the stories directly (the hero’s journey, etc.)

Obviously I could be wrong :)

Sincerely
Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: two kinds of lucid dreaming
replies.content:
When I was a child, I had a period when I was having anxiety dreams about school night after night. I would dream I was at school, and having a bad day, nothing exotic, just mundane school problems. I wasn't getting enough rest because I would be going to school for real and then dreaming I was in school at night.

So I taught myself how to recognize that I was having a dream and not actually in school. I don't remember exactly how I did this, but I think it was just by setting an intention when I was in bed waiting to fall asleep. After I started to "wake up" in the dream and be aware that I was dreaming, I began to control the dream so that nothing unpleasant happened in it. Once I got to the stage where I could make my dreams about school pleasant instead of anxious, they were sort of boring and I stopped dreaming about school regularly.

I didn't know lucid dreaming was a thing, so after I solved the immediate problem, I got out of the habit. There was no one I could discuss it with.

Subsequently I have had recurring dreams in dream-familiar locations, and now and then I have anxious falling, being chased, traffic accident dreams and the like, but I have not had the experience of having unpleasant dreams with the same theme night after night, so I haven't gotten back to lucid dreaming.

One exception: if I lie around in bed in the morning half asleep, I often go into a state where I dream that I can fly or levitate (bob about like a balloon). The sensation is familiar enough that I can recognize I'm having that kind of dream again. Then I try to get better control of the ability to rise and move around. I rise up through the ceilings of rooms, try to move in a particular direction instead of drifting, etc. There is a description in Alice in Wonderland of Alice putting her hand on the balustrade and sort of floating down the stairs, and that's what it feels like. It feels easy and natural to do it close to the ground, but if I'm outdoors and decide to fly high up in the sky like a bird, it frightens me a little and I have to concentrate harder not to fall.

I presume this is taking place somewhere on the lower astral. Every time I have this dream, I practice flying and try to get better at it, but I can't say I've made much progress.

I think what induces this psychic state is being very comfortable in a warm bed with soft bedclothes. I think the effect is something like being in a flotation tank; I'm not feeling the pull of gravity and my skin is not being stimulated, so I'm in partial sensory deprivation and my mind goes somewhere else.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi, and thanks!

So I've been meditating on the Tarot bit of Malkuth in Paths of Wisdom, which brings up one of the things I've wondered about for a while: namely, the fact that most interpretations of Swords and, to a lesser extent, Wands cards skew more negative than those for Cups and Pentacles. It seems to conflict with the notion that each suit represents an element and/or the its associations (maybe it's just the WASP in me, for example, but I don't think intellect is any more or less destructive than emotion), so I'm curious whether this reflects a deeper pattern or is just a quirk between two methods of interpretation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The various schemes of interpreting the Tarot reflect cultural shifts and fashions, of course. For a long time now magic has been embraced by the romantic end of the romantic/classical dialectic in our culture, and so most Tarot books reflect the romantic distrust of intellect and embrace of emotion. If history had turned out differently, and magic rather than science had become the core project of our culture, your average Tarot book would tend to give favorable interpretations of swords and unfavorable interpretations of cups!

This is one of the reasons I encourage people to cast daily readings and compare those readings to the events of the day. That kind of relentless reality checking is a good way to correct for cultural biases of this kind.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent--thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This makes a lot of sense. From a classical rather than a romantic perspective, cups could be characterized as the suit of illusions and swords as the suit of the destruction of illusions.

Meanwhile wands are the suit of idealism while pentacles are the suit of pragmatism. Neither is 'positive' or 'negative'; you need both in balance.

This is a helpful conceptual re-balancing for me in my own Tarot work. Thank you!

- Dylan

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've noticed something along these lines in my practice with Geomancy. So far, at least, Rubeus hasn't been nearly as malefic a potent in my daily readings as you'd expect from traditional geomantic manuals. I suspect this difference may be partly due to the much greater suspicion with which medieval people regarded intemperance, indulgence, and rowdiness.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Rubeus gets a bad rap. In my readings, it's the figure of energy, passion, and involvement in life; it's problematic in the first house, because it tells you that you (or the querent, if you're doing a reading for someone else) are too deeply involved in the situation to be able to assess it clearly. Other than that, though, it's not especially troublesome.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This may be trivial... Anyhow - does it even make any sense at all to assign a general positive or negative tendency to certain cards? Over the time I got the feeling that it depends strongly on the context and position of a card. Taking the 9 of swords (Waite/Smith) as an example: Possibly not very nice if it depicts a state. Anyhow, if you asked for what kind of action would lead to a positive result in some matter and draw this card, I'd probably interpret it as a strong call for action. Still it seems to imply a negative context for the past or present, yet since the card is in a position of advice for action it seems to get a different spin altogether.

Is this a valid approach? Is there any danger of whitewashing?

Greetings again,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a very sensible approach to things. Value judgments are always personal and always relative to context, so, yes, the 9 of Swords can be a good thing -- it can mean doubt and suspicion, for example, and there are times when doubt and suspicion are a good policy; it can also mean harshness and absence of pity, and there are times when those are necessary, too.

comments.title: Astral - how strong could/should the impressions be?
comments.content:
Hi JMG, hope you are well!

About 8 years ago I worked hard to astral project, and eventually succeeded to an incredible degree with a fully embodied experience in another place (vaguely modern city, conversations, flying (of course)). It was amazing, but a couple of experiences seemed to quench my thirst and since then I felt that mostly, my work is here, and the effort required to get to the astral isn't worth it. The lasting result was that I know there is an Unseen, and that has made my life Golden.

But now, having wandered various paths I end up at this door of good old Western High Magic. I found Dion Fortune's books absolutely wonderful, also yours. I marinate for days at a time in the Tree, and what I see in the world mixes with coalescing forms in my imagination to make a glorious conversation that seems to continue after I sleep (I'll wake up with the sense of voices suddenly (though kindly) stilled).

All good, and normal I guess, among this interesting crowd!

Here is where I feel a sense of crossroads, and some thoughts from you or other interested readers may help.

Nowadays, my sense of the Astral is very light. Mental pictures formed during the Middle Pillar Exercise. I take it on faith that I am "there" in a "real place," though another part of me complains that these are only daydreams. I am not sure about trying to deepen those sense because I'm leery of "astralism," by which I mean a fascination with astral realms for their own sake...I might be wasting energy that should better be concentrated on the still voice, which does not communicate with visions, but which I do feel.

However, so so so much of this Magic(k) tradition is concerned with the Astral and Etheric. The temptation there for me is one of "Really Knowing," for example, in the ways Dion Fortune would receive messages to talk to a Colonel X or Mr. Y, and all of this would be duly confirmed.

Is it a sin (by which I mean a waste of time, a distraction) to search for certainty in dealing with the Unseen? Is this an activity which, if one succeeds at it, ultimately distracts you from union with the Higher? Could it be that in becoming a mechanic of the Unseen there is as much loss as gain?

I guess I feel that this is the direction I will pursue but I have a sense of a sad kind of danger over the space. I guess I'm talking about the damage that Control may do. Maybe an analogy is that of the scientist who began on a thrilling quest for the secrets of life, and ended up very "wise" amid learned dissertations -- erudite but lifeless. His heaven irretrievably lost decades before when he rode too hard for his dream.

Thanks!
--M

replies.title: Re: Astral - how strong could/should the impressions be?
replies.content:
The best advice I ever got along these lines was that it's unhelpful to chase after astral experience and unhelpful to ignore it. As you proceed in your magical training, you'll work on exercises such as scrying in the spirit vision that are intended to develop the capacity of astral vision, and your previous experience with astral projection and your astral experiences in sleep will make the work easy and the results prompt in arriving.

The tendency to fall into dualism -- to see astral and spiritual experience as opposed to one another, rather than each having their own valid place in a complete system of inner training -- is widespread but counterproductive. Here as elsewhere, the opposite of one bad idea is usually another bad idea, and equilibrium is the Royal Secret.

replies.title: Re: Astral - how strong could/should the impressions be?
replies.content: Somehow your answer provides relief, thanks much!

comments.title: Banishing vs Invoking
comments.content: I’ve been practicing the Sphere of Protection and the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, but only doing banishing, for about a year now. I just started the Middle Pillar exercise last week. My way of memorizing, btw, is to copy out by hand the instructions and then leave my notebook in the circle with me in case I need it. I’m not doing the invoking part though. To be clear, is that exactly the same ritual only with a clockwise pentagram? And where should I put it in the order? I want to make sure I have all the elements in place before I start with the temple.

replies.title: Re: Banishing vs Invoking
replies.content:
Okay, which version of the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram are you doing? If it's the version taught in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn and my essays here on Dreamwidth, yes, you start from the same point (the top point of the pentagram) and trace clockwise to invoke rather than counterclockwise.

You use the invoking ritual to open a space of interaction with magical powers and the banishing ritual to close it. You don't include the invoking ritual in your daily practice -- that's the banishing ritual followed by the Middle Pillar. You do it when you open a temple, which you should do once or twice a week for the time being. You can also practice the invoking ritual by itself, and in that case it can be helpful to follow it with the banishing ritual, so you begin to develop a sense of how the two rituals complement each other.

replies.title: Re: Banishing vs Invoking
replies.content:
1) I'm working slowly through the CGD, at first erratically but now more consistently, trying to progress in order (contrary to my natural inclination). My question regards adding the Middle Pillar exercise before completing the Ovate training. You've said to follow exactly for best results. Does that apply to the MP ritual? What are the up and downsides of jumping ahead and adding it into daily practice while catching up on divination and meditation practices?

2) I'd be very interested in your thoughts on a group working along the lines of Dion Fortune's "Magical Battle of Britain" but for re-enchanting Earth, as you so marvelously begin the DMH.

Thank you for all you do, your work is a great blessing.

replies.title: Re: Banishing vs Invoking
replies.content:
1) Nope. When you're training out of a book, follow the course of training exactly as given, setting aside the temptation to think that you know more than the author and therefore can rearrange things at will, and you'll get the best results.

2) It's certainly an option, but it's one to approach with due caution. Beware of the tendency, which is present in all of us, to want to play at being superheroes saving a passive and helpless world. The world is neither passive nor helpless; it's not actually disenchanted at all -- it's just that some human beings have blinded themselves to the vast enchantments that surround them at every moment. To do a working along Fortune's lines is to put yourself at the disposal of powers far greater than you are, in order to further a shared purpose. Worth doing? Of course, so long as you remember that it's not about you.

Oh, and you'll have to come face to face with anything you do that furthers the condition of disenchantment, and with any benefits you receive from the disenchantment, but that's hardwired into any working of this kind.

replies.title: Re: Banishing vs Invoking
replies.content: Thank you, makes sense. A few years ago a huge supercell was wreaking havok over most of the us Midwest. We live in a dilapidating but sturdy century old farmhouse in tornado alley. It was night and while lying on my bed debating whether to stay up (I sleep thru anything) I tried tuning into the spirit of the storm (I imagined Gaia) - felt distinctly female and I got the distinct impression She was in a proper rage - normally I struggle with those sorts of communications but maybe the intensity of the storm (very positively energizing usually) boosted my imagination. Lacking anything better to do I sent out the biggest wave of love I could muster for awhile, then drifted asleep. Next day my sceptic spouse said "boy that storm was weird, on radar the area near our house was completely quiet while everything around suffered damages." My one and only encounter but yes, I understand we are infintessimally small and weak. We have done as much as able since 2005 to "collapse now and avoid the rush" and to work toward positive impacts but been seemingly slow and imperfect progress.

replies.title: Re: Banishing vs Invoking
replies.content: That's a good traditional way of responding to the storm spirits, and it very often gets good results.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello, JMG.

This might be a bit of a silly question, but it is something which I have been mulling over lately and couldn't find a satisfactory answer.

Having studied astrology for a few years, I decided a while ago to make one of the Picatrix talismans. It worked very well and fast, so much so that it prompted me to think if there is any sort of 'hidden cost' in this kind of (selfish but not harmful to others) magic. That is, if by using magic to make things easier for me now, that will, by some sort of law of balance, make things harder in the future? Or is magic just like any other tool?

Thanks!

Sorry if I'm unclear, I'm not used to writing in English.

Paulo B

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Magic is just like any other tool. If you help yourself in a way that doesn't harm others, you're simply going to attract energies that help you help yourself and not harm others, and as those energies radiate from you, they'll encourage others to help themselves in ways that don't harm you.

It's very much like the karmic implications of honest work. If you work hard and prosper in some honest trade, you're adding to the sum total of wealth in the world; you benefit, others benefit, and the world benefits. If you seek wealth in a way that makes other people poorer, sure, you're going to face blowback in this or another life, but if you're providing through your efforts a good or a service that people want and need, and they exchange the product of their labors (directly or, via money, indirectly) with you so that they can benefit from the good or service you provide, then everyone benefits.

comments.title: Cgd tree working
comments.content:
I also have a question about tree working, which I have just begun a few days ago. Is it better to stick with one tree or try several? I'm having a positive experience with the tree I'm working with,so I was planning on staying with this tree for a good while then trying some other trees to see how the feeling differs. I feel like if I were to switch around from tree to tree too quickly it might mess things up: is that a concern at all?

It's a subtle feeling, kind of like how two magnets put together affect each other's fields. It's also a very vertical feeling somehow.
Thank you,
Wooler

replies.title: Re: Cgd tree working
replies.content:
Follow your intuition, and keep working with that tree until you can feel that it's time to work with others. Trees benefit from the exchange of energy, just as humans do; it may be that the tree you're working with needs the energy you give it, or can use that energy for some purpose of its own.

Your description is quite accurate, btw -- I suspect you either have a lot of skill working with etheric energy, or will be able to develop that skill easily.

replies.title: Re: Cgd tree working
replies.content:
Can you please briefly outline the method(s) of tree working and/or point me towards some reference material? I manage a small woodlot and want to develop better listening habits.
Many thanks.

Gawain

replies.title: Re: Cgd tree working
replies.content: There are various ways of doing tree workings, but all of them involve placing your energy body and the tree's energy body in proximity and letting energy flow between them. You can find an article on one method here, and others in my books The Druidry Handbook and The Celtic Golden Dawn.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What do you think of astrocartography? A legitimate supplement to a natal chart, or new age gobbledygook?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not really quite either. To my mind it's useful but oversimplified. The way I handle relocational astrology is to cast my natal chart for the date and time of my birth, corrected as needed for the change in time zones, but for the place I have in mind. That way you have the identical planetary positions but different ascendant, midheaven, and houses; by comparing that to my actual natal chart, I can see what the differences will be and judge accordingly. For example, one relocation chart I considered before leaving Seattle put a malefic planet right on the descendant, where it would be in opposition to my rising degree -- bad idea! Another put a benefic planet close to my midheaven, and when I made that move, why, my career took a sharp turn for the better.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This looks like a very straightforward technique; thank you!

What you describe here makes it sound as though the new ascendent and houses have either supplemented, or replaced, those from the nativity cast for your actual birthplace. (It also seems related to the practice of moving a nation's capital in order to get a more favorable mundane chart.) Is that accurate?

And if this is fairly close, how does this affect your astrological practice when it comes to progressed charts, synastries, and the like? Do you stick with your actual birthplace for those charts, or update them to the place where you now make your home?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The relocation chart doesn't actually supplant your natal chart -- you were still born where you were born, and your energy body took on the imprint of the cosmos where and when it did -- but it modifies the way the energies of the chart express themselves. Progressed charts, since they're based on the birth chart, take the place of birth as their location; on the other hand, solar return charts and the like, in my experience, are best done for the place where you actually are when the return happens.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I just cast a chart for somewhere I'm planning to move to, and found it seems to match me a lot. Curiously, it seems like it's become a better fit over time, and especially since I decided I would move there. Is it possible that the fact I will move there is having an astrological impact on me today?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm not sure it's an astrological impact -- it may simply be that your intention to move there is having a magical effect.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Can you do this the other way round - find out where the astrology is best first, then see if moving there is an option?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, indeed you can. The one thing to keep in mind in doing that is that there's usually more than one way to get a favorable relocation -- depending on your chart, for example, if you want success, you can put your natal sun at your midheaven or your natal Jupiter at your midheaven, and either one will have a good effect.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi Mr. Greer,

In your book _Natural Magic_, you talk about consecrating talismans. Is that the same as enchanting them, or are the intentions/methods different?

I currently work with the magic system in the DMH, so I understand how to enchant in a grove ritual, but I will be starting work with CGD soon. Is this something you can do with CGD rituals?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's the same thing. I simply used a different term in the Druid book.

2) Yes, The Celtic Golden Dawn includes rituals for consecrating amulets and working tools. You'll have to put in at least a year of hard work before you get there, though, because that comes in the Druid Grade section.

replies.title: Sigils
replies.content:
After listening to your lively discussion about the role of magic in the Kek Wars https://www.stitcher.com/podcast/emerge/e/57187347?autoplay=true I checked out the linked primer of chaos magic including making sigils-as-servitors. Your talk mentioned using sigils in a seemingly positive light, and I know you have said you don't use servitors. Is a sigil a form of talisman? Are sigils useful and relatively safe for neophytes, and if so, in which books of yours (or recommended) is this covered?

For those following DMH or CGD - since creating sigils doesn't appear to be covered, is it correct to assume they aren't that important and/or useful in the big scheme of all things magical?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Sigils
replies.content:
Sigils are a specialized technique. I don't find it especially useful, and the particular magical traditions in which I've been trained don't use it, so I tend to use other tools for practical workings, and my books don't cover it. On the other hand, a lot of people in the Chaos Magic scene use sigils with good effect; you'll want to ask them for good sources.

A talisman is a physical object charged with magical energies. You use a talisman when you want a continuous magical pressure toward some goal over the long term; once you make and consecrate it, it just keeps on going like that bunny in the ad. My most successful workings have used talismans, so it's something I tend to teach a lot!

replies.title: Re: Sigils
replies.content: Thank you! I'll look forward to eventually reaching that part of the book.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Great. Thank you so much!

comments.title: English Cousin
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Last Thursday I woke very early from a nightmare of my cousin killing his wife in a very bloody and distressing act. The same morning, I received a letter from him saying he has been having nightmares for about the last 6 months of something attacking him. This is the same time period I have been having problems with him fantasizing about me.

He has several times struck his wife during these dreams and a few times has woken up with his hands around her neck, pushing her away. He asked me what was going on. I had already warned him about the danger he was putting himself in and said again he is contacting something dark and possibly dangerous.

I recommended he take a Hoodoo bath at least once a week, more often if he is feeling weak, stressed or lustful. I told him to put a saucer of vinegar in his bedroom and to pray before bed and put himself under the protection of whatever saint or angel he thought would help. I also said he should give up having any lustful thoughts of me.

I continue to distance myself from him and banish my attraction to him in my SOP. Is there anything else you would recommend to help him?
Yours gratefully,
Maxine Rogers

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content: I recommend that you have as little as possible to do with him, and that includes providing him with help. Prayer's about as much as I'd encourage you to do.

replies.title: Re: English Cousin
replies.content:
Thank you. I will do as you say. Many thanks.
Maxine

comments.title: Working for lost object
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Could you suggest a working to help me find a lost earring? I have the other of that pair, if it helps.

Thank you for Magic Mondays! I have benefited from your responses to other people.

replies.title: Re: Working for lost object
replies.content: Start by opening a magical space using any appropriate ritual, such as the Sphere of Protection.  Enter into a meditative state, holding the other earring in one hand. Imagine yourself calling a spirit to assist you in finding the earring and, still in imagination, see what shows up. Have a conversation with the being who answers, and see what you learn. Close in appropriate ritual form.

replies.title: Re: Working for lost object
replies.content: Thank you, I'll try this!

replies.title: Re: Working for lost object
replies.content:
Interesting. Would this work as well for a non-paired object? Say, a book, or a ring?

-Anthony

replies.title: Re: Working for lost object
replies.content: Good question. Try it and see!

replies.title: Re: Working for lost object
replies.content: Will do! ☺️

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good day sir!

Thank you as always for this opportunity.

When you described the way that you do the circulation of light as part of the sphere of protection, it reminded me of the rings cosmos, chaos, and pass-not. Am I reading that right?

I have been working on the elemental cross and circulation of light for a month or so. Today I noticed that when I was done the area felt calmer or "more settled". Is that what you are pointing at when you said I should be feeling that something is different in the space?

Thank you,
Matt

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) As far as I know, the person who invented the Sphere of Protection didn't know about the Cos.Doc., but yes, I've noted the similarity too.

2) Good! Yes, that's a common way of beginning to sense the effect. Congratulations; you've put in the time and effort, and are beginning to get the results.

comments.title: Regional Gods
comments.content:
JMG-

Thanks again for doing this. Last week I wrote in about thanking the archangel Gabriel and I took your advice, so far I think it's gone well. Anyway, I have a followup of sorts.

I mentioned in that question that I'd just moved to Mexico and didn't have a sense of gods around here, which is still true, but I've been reading a good bit about the pre-contact religions (some of it via the Lewis Spence link you posted a while back) as well as the blending of religions that comes after. My question is, there's just no getting around the fact that there was a tremendous amount of human sacrifice happening around here. I've seen quite a few theories on this, one of the more recent being that there's a chemical in corn which seems to increase human proclivity for violence, which is interesting, but skirts another possibility that's not popular these says: the gods around here wanted human sacrifice.

I'm curious if...
1) you have any thoughts on why human sacrifice happens
2) it would be advisable to "reach out" to local gods/beings/what have you in a place where you don't know the area or culture
3) what's the best way to protect oneself from beings that turn out to be not friendly in the above scenario (I have a GD practice, but I've only been doing it for a little over a year)

replies.title: Re: Regional Gods
replies.content:
1) Some gods ask for it. I don't hang out with those gods.

2) I'd say learn something about the culture first.

3) Daily banishing rituals and weekly performance of the Rose Cross ritual ought to do the job.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I just finished doing a quick first read of your Natural Magic Encyclopedia—I'm excited to get started! (I'm the person who asked a while back about combining NME practice with DMH practice, not to mention the person who brought up voor last week, if you like to keep vague track of your commenters ;-)

I wonder how you would go about adding new plants, such as Maple, Pine, and Sassafras? Research, surely, such as herbals for the astrological correspondences, and any folk and magical lore.

Possibly also researching traditional spells that use the item, and reserse engineering its purpose?

Also, would you recommend scrying in the spirit vision, and asking the plant how it could be used? If so, how would you go about that within the DMH tradition- imagine a dolmen arch with the plant growing next to it, or sprigs hanging above the arch?

And do you ever do the equivalent of scientific testing with natural magic? Such as laying out a series of workings, from which you can see which works better?

Another question in this strain: would traditional American/hoodoo correspondences and uses be considered essentially more research and development for the natural magic tradition? I noticed that you include some of this in NME, such as High John the Conqueror and the use of floor washes, and there is a lot more that could be explored here. Prosperity, in particular, seems to have not much presence within the NME, while hoodoo has several approaches including plants with a focus in this area. I'm not necessarily trying to develop a Universal Natural Magic system, just one that works well for me, and utilizes my southern Indiana resources appropriately.

Thanks once again, and for all your previous answers!

-Anthony

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I think you've answered most of your own questions! Yes, yes, and again yes. In scrying, I'd pass through a dolmen arch into an imaginal forest, go looking for the plant in question, and when I found it, sit with it and talk to it.

As for the fusion of hoodoo and European magic, that's taking place already, so you might as well join in the work. When I wrote the NME, I had only the most basic exposure to hoodoo -- that was well before I studied with Cat Yronwode. If I were to rewrite it I'd cover a lot more hoodoo.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

I want to stir around a health related question here so I'll fully accept it if you decide to moderate it out.

Let's say, I stubbed a toe badly. Obviously, I will go to doctors first and follow their advise to heal the injury. Once that is taken care of, would it be helpful to go scrying and talk to my stubbed toe or the injury itself to see what was going on there on the more subtle levels?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Give it a try and see how it works!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Haha!


Yep, more than fair enough. I'm definitely happy to experiment with this. At what point within the Celtic Golden Dawn training would you recommend to start trying this out? Would it be Druid grade?

I attempted scrying a few dozen times early in the Ovate grade. The results were mixed - some really good, some rather far from good - to the point of being scary. I realised that I lack some inner skills that are necessary for it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, that'd be a Druid grade practice.

comments.title: Chart
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Is your birth data public and if so may I learn what it is?

replies.title: Re: Chart
replies.content: No, it isn't public, and no, you may not. One of the disadvantages to the rediscovery of traditional astrological magic is that there are some extremely nasty things you can do to someone if you know certain details of their natal chart; it's possible to defend against those, and I know how to do it, but it's a lot of work.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I've read a few variations on how to make and use a witch bottle. How would you recommend doing it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd say choose a recipe that appeals to you and give it a try. I use different protective workings, so don't have any personal experience with that one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What could be an effective natural magic working for academic success? And to make myself more loveable?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Why, that's something you should work out for yourself. You'll find all the necessary information in my book The Natural Magic Encyclopedia, and in the process of figuring it out, you'll learn skills that you can then apply to a wide range of magical workings.

My primary reason for hosting these question and answer sessions is to encourage people to learn and practice magic; if the handful of workings I've described here in detail inspire you to learn and practice magic yourself, why, all the better. ;-)

comments.title: Krampus Day
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I moved to Vienna last year and Krampus day is coming up. My friends warned me not to be out alone that night because drunk men dressed as Krampus like to scare people. Other than that precaution, is there anything you think I should do? What do you think of Krampus?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Krampus Day
replies.content:
Ah, Krampus! I'd encourage you to ask your Austrian friends what people used to do to honor Krampus, and do it if you can. I have a great deal of affection for Krampus; it seems to me that if more children were scared of being popped into his wicker basket and taken away into the night, never to be seen again, there would be fewer sniveling spoiled brats in the world...

(Yes, I know. Bah, humbug!)

replies.title: Re: Krampus Day
replies.content: I recommend Brom’s novel Krampus, The Yule Lord.

replies.title: Re: Krampus Day
replies.content: It's well worth reading!

comments.title: Human inventions
comments.content:
Hi adjmg,

I noticed that in some of the Old Testament that it was stated that non human intelligences gave man metallurgy, makeup and other things ( including occult knowledge . I also noticed that not only issac newton, but the inventor of tv and the inventor of modern rocket engines dabbled in the occult. Is there a connection between the occult and human technology? Or Am I wrongly making a connection?

Dashui

replies.title: Re: Human inventions
replies.content: If the basic thesis of occultism is correct, magic is always present everywhere, and so everything that exists has a connection to occultism. Our current industrial technology may be remembered a long time from now as an unusually clumsy and limited, though very powerful, form of natural magic...

comments.title: Enchanted ponds and pools
comments.content:
Another question, if I may: the DMH talks about enchanting standing stones, as an advanced magical and druidical practice. While reading Dion Fortune's Goat Foot God, there was a passing reference to "hammer-pools" in a way that puts them in a similar category as standing stones. Do you know anything about these?

This also got me thinking- it may be nice to enchant/consecrate a pond, similarly to standing stones! Is this a thing (yet)?

Thanks again!
-Anthony

replies.title: Re: Enchanted ponds and pools
replies.content: I don't recall that bit of Fortune's novel -- will have to reread it sometime and doublecheck. Certainly pools and ponds played a role in the archaic ley system, but how to enchant them is an interesting question, since liquid water won't hold onto an enchantment the way that good paramagnetic stone will.

replies.title: Re: Enchanted ponds and pools
replies.content:
The reference was just in passing, towards the beginning.

Interesting about the enchantment method. I suppose you could sink large stones into the pond?

replies.title: Re: Enchanted ponds and pools
replies.content: The problem is that cold water is a very effective eraser of magical energies, so the stones would lose their charge quickly. It's an intriguing question!

replies.title: Re: Enchanted ponds and pools
replies.content:
Looking at this a couple of days later, what about putting in the contents of a fluid condenser made from stream-sourced gold nuggets, while leaving some nugget bits in the pool for occasions when more condenser is needed?

It would provide a backstory for the concept of "wishing wells", when occult theory suggests that numismatic metals and pools of water don't work that way.

replies.title: Re: hammer-pools
replies.content:
I don't have an Oxford English Dictionary at home, and I don't want to take the time right now to look up the passage in the Goat Foot God, but FWIW, I suggest that a hammer-pool is a place where a smith would set up a forge and quench the metal after heating it and hammering it on an anvil.

If that happens to be true, then the magical association would be with smithcraft.

comments.title: Lucid dreaming
comments.content:
A friend of mine recently started to practice lucid dreaming and already got a little of success. His motivation is mainly to have fun - anyhow I started to ask myself on how this would interfere with magic. Is lucid dreaming practiced by mages? Is this combination advisable? What would happen if somebody performed a ritual inside a lucid dream (if this is possible at all...)?

Thank you for the opportunity to ask!

Even more greetings from Nachtgurke ;)

replies.title: Re: Lucid dreaming
replies.content: I've done rituals inside of nightmares -- your common or garden variety nightmare will back down pretty fast if faced with the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram! -- so it's certainly an option. I haven't really pursued the subject, though, so don't know how well it would work in general.

comments.title: Dealing with the representatives of a bureaucracy that don't share my worldview...
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A brief background a brief background of our issue. My wife and I had a daughter six months ago. We were attempting to have a natural birth at a birth center (our first child was a home birth went wrong that ended in a cesarean section). We ended up, much to our dismay, giving birth in a children's hospital after some minor complications that necessitated our transfer. Luckily, our birth went off in the hospital without a hitch. Unluckily, a neonatal nurse practitioner with an agenda against our obstetrician and out of hospital births (that we were not aware of) landed us in the neonatal intensive care unit (nicu).

A traumatic five days in the nicu followed. To be brief, it involved our child being treated expressly against our wishes, conflict with the attending physician, threats of child protective services being called, and meetings with the director of the nicu. The hospital eventually bent to our will and we were discharged early, rather apparently because they were way beyond the line of legal wrong doing.

Fast forward to the coming week, my wife and I have a meeting with members of the hospital administration to discuss these issues. Neither of us have any interest in filing a lawsuit or in doing anything but moving on with our lives, but we feel that we have a duty to try and prevent this sort of thing from happening to others. A significant part of our reasoning is we are middle class, outspoken, and both hold advanced degrees in science; if they are willing to treat us in such a manner, how are they treating others? Our goals are to have the hospital acknowledge their wrong doing, have the hospital drop the costs from the days in the nicu where we would have left but they wouldn't let us, and take concrete steps to prevent things like this from happening to others in the future (basically, to abide by their own written code of conduct with respect to patients).

We are heathens, I associate with Germanic/Norse deities and wife generally associates with Celtic deities. Neither of us are practiced enough in ritual magic to attempt anything of that sort, but is there some sort of more basic magical action we could take to try and bolster our chances of success? I've thought of appealing to Odin, because of his place as god of wisdom and the spoken word or to Tyr, because he is god of justice, but I'm not sure whether a direct appeal would be appropriate, or if there is something else we should do.

Any advice is greatly appreciated.

Thank you,
HP

replies.title: Re: Dealing with the representatives of a bureaucracy that don't share my worldview...
replies.content:
Make offerings to the gods and ask them for their help in this matter. This is the kind of thing about which people have been invoking gods and goddesses as far back as records go! As Heathens, you doubtless know traditional ways to make offerings to your deities, and those will be very suitable in this case. Remember that you can call on multiple deities -- that's one of the benefits of polytheism -- so by all means call on Odin and Tyr, and any other deities who are appropriate, and encourage your wife to call on whichever Celtic deities are appropriate. (If she works with the Welsh deities, I'd put Rhiannon at the head of that particular list, since she had a child taken from her and tends to be very protective of those who've had similar experiences.)

One practical piece of advice: let the hospital know that if a satisfactory resolution can't be reached, you will go to the media. The medical industry fears nothing as much as it fears having its misbehavior exposed to the public eye. I once got fraudulently billed by a hospital, which refused to back down even though I could prove that they were charging me for services I hadn't received; divination suggested going to the media, among other ways of dealing with things; I contacted the local TV station's consumer affairs segment, got three or four minutes on the air, and proceeded to watch in mordant amusement as the hospital hurriedly dropped the bill and tried to kiss my butt.

Oh, and that hospital no longer exists. I don't know that the workings I did were solely responsible for that, but I wasn't displeased.

replies.title: Re: Dealing with the representatives of a bureaucracy that don't share my worldview...
replies.content: Thank you! I appreciate the advice and I'll echo several other comments I've seen by saying that I glean a great deal from this forum, my wife and I are very grateful that you provide it.

replies.title: Re: Dealing with the representatives of a bureaucracy that don't share my worldview...
replies.content: My partner (and I) had a similar experience when our first child was born. We complained and got a meeting and a 20page letter of apology which didn’t really contain much by way of apology, at which point we had a long and thoughtful discussion at home and decided to drop it, and, if we still felt we needed to “do something”, to direct our energy into supporting other people having similar experiences. We thought a lot about the reasons the system struggled to deal with our complaint in any meaningful way and came to think that the system was very unlikely to change or even reflect usefully as a result of our complaint. My advice would be to fight the good fight as far as you feel you need to fight it, but don’t worry about fixing the whole world. You might get incredibly lucky and find yourself talking to someone with the ability to understand where you’re coming from, or someone with power to alter the system elements which troubled you, but you’re more likely to meet with people who feel sure that, if anything even did go wrong for you, it was an anomaly and not a systemic issue, or indeed that you are the issue and not the system. So I say see how you get on but try not to pin your hopes for “closure” on succeeding in fixing anything in our medicalised maternity processes. The real closure will probably come elsewhere — this meeting is just something you feel obliged to do as good people. But whatever you decide to do I wanted to wish you the best for getting through a tough time and learning your new life together.

replies.title: Re: Dealing with the representatives of a bureaucracy that don't share my worldview...
replies.content:
I wholeheartedly agree with everything your saying, we really aren't crusaders thinking we'll change the course of the medical industry. It is abundantly clear to us that our differences resulted from fundamentally different worldviews than the average provider of modern medicine.

Really, I think doing this is more about closure for us and forcing them, whether or not it does any good, to have to reflect on things. As you said, they are unlikely to change or even reflect usefully as result... but as my grandfather said, "sling enough mud at the wall and sooner or later some of it'll stick". So, maybe we won't have any effect immediately, but it is still a drop in the bucket (at least I hope).

Thank you for the well wishes!
